title: Women in the Art
subtitle: The female practitioners the record kept — and how few it kept
directive: map the women of alchemy across the whole tradition

narrative.intro: Maria the Jewess, whose bain-marie still carries her name; Theosebeia, Zosimos's addressee and collaborator; Hildegard's convent natural philosophy; Anna Zieglerin staking her life on the lion's blood at a ducal court. A handful of dots spanning fourteen centuries — and that is precisely the exhibit: the tradition had women throughout, and the record kept almost none of them.
narrative.provenance_note: Persons = the five women with events in the corpus (8 events total, 3rd–16th c.). The sparsity is the subject: it measures the gendered survival of evidence, not the historical participation of women. Kleopatra the Alchemist is in the corpus vocabulary but carries no dated, geocoded event, so she cannot appear — which proves the point. Borders shown are c. 500 and fit only the late-antique stops; treat them as backdrop, not claim.
